chapters.title: Great things start small
chapters.text:
The road to self-discovery can be thorny and tortuous. It often involves confronting and overcoming personal flaws. Strangely, to unlock your true potential and build self-respect, you must first experience disappointment in yourself. Sometimes, hitting a dead end can be the key to finding the right path forward.

It happened to Scott Harrison. After a decade of an immoral riotous lifestyle, he developed a profound charitable mission. His inspiring story can offer hope to even the most desperate people.

Harrison was one of New York City's most successful nightclub promoters. His routine consisted of endless parties, alcohol, drugs, and promiscuity. He did not particularly care for Christian values and principles until his health suddenly failed. The possibility of death made him rethink his life and commit to making the most of it. He participated in charity for the first time as a compromise with his conscience. However, his life was never the same after he knew the incomparable joy of selflessly assisting others.

Altruism turns into a fulfilling reward rather than a sacrifice when you see how it changes others' lives for the better.

The brainchild of Scott Harrison, charity:water, is a global organization born from the courage and audacity of one kind-hearted dreamer. His dedication and enthusiasm attracted thousands of like-minded people. His good intentions, strengthened by his faith in God, have already given 16 million people a chance for a better life; many more are yet to come.

Follow along and learn how Scott Harrison went from being frustrated with life to finding a new purpose in helping and serving those in need. Let this story convince you that faith can work miracles when supported by action and diligence!

chapters.title: The world is teeming with wonders and dangers
chapters.text:
Scott Harrison first encountered the fragility of the human body when he was still a child. His mother fell ill and rapidly worsened, but the doctors could not diagnose her. As it later turned out, she had carbon monoxide poisoning. This exposure led to fainting, dizziness, weakness, and heightened her risk of a heart attack.

Clinical ecologists treated Scott's mother. They explained that the body accumulates allergens and can efficiently deal with them when there are few. However, due to his mother's chemical exposure, her immunity to external pathogens was virtually zero. The only way to save her was to protect her from contamination and place her in sterile conditions. She needed fresh air, healthy food, and, above all, clean water.

Scott's father specifically went to the artesian well to fetch spring water, as it was the only safe option for her. Scott watched in awe as his father filled heavy glass containers with clean water. For a moment, he believed that water was a gift from God to humanity.

People rarely realize the planet's wonders; one of them is that clean water is hiding deep in the earth, right beneath their feet.

Scott's mother was defenseless against the aggressive environment, so the eight-year-old Scott learned to protect her. He checked the cleanliness of the premises before his mother entered and warned her if he smelled anything potentially dangerous. Moreover, since his father was busy with work and his mother had to avoid certain products and household chemicals, Scott started making significant contributions to cooking and cleaning. The responsibility that fell on him made him feel pride and joy that he could help his family. These early experiences laid the foundation for the altruism Scott would later channel into his charitable work over the years.

However, he had to navigate a difficult adolescence and rebellious youth to reach that future. When he entered high school, he felt the difference between his family and others and was conflicted. He loved his mother and felt sorry for her, but at the same time, he understood that her illness had become a personal trap and misfortune for him. It sparked the teen's problematic behavior, leading to negative influences and unhealthy habits.

chapters.title: It's never too late to turn things around
chapters.text:
Scott's family was deeply religious, and his parents tried to raise their son as a disciplined, highly moral person. However, the more they restricted him, the more freedom he craved. Dreaming of escaping his small hometown, he seized the first opportunity that came his way. At 19, he announced his plans to his parents to move to New York City. He also informed them that he would not go to college but would play in a musical group and forge his own path. His efforts did not make him a rock star but earned him a place among the city's most accomplished club workers and notorious partygoers. With the perverse pleasure of being outlawed by a religious family, he didn't hide his sinful lifestyle from his parents. But regardless of how much he shocked them, they never gave up on him; they continued reaching out and communicating with him. They prayed to God, hoping their son would come to his senses.

The more famous Scott became, the less morality remained in his life. The more profit he made, the more he spent on drugs and useless entertainment. And, of course, his health deteriorated over time. However, it is often the case that individuals begin to value their health only after losing it. And it was not until Scott's limbs started to fail that he finally snapped out of the illusion of his invincibility. He went to the doctor and underwent all possible examinations, but they could not find anything abnormal. When medicine doesn't give people the answers they want, they often turn to God. As for the “prodigal son,” this meant returning home to his pious parents.

So, concern for his health forced the almost thirty-year-old Scott to rethink his lifestyle. Upon reflecting on the past decade, he realized that he had become the worst version of himself. He had surrounded himself with worthless false values ​​and superficial friendships. His relationships had no love, and his work had no meaning. Scott couldn’t remember the last time he felt healthy without drugs and when he was last proud of his actions. These thoughts led him to remember how, as a child, he served his mother and stood guard over her peace. Now he had to find a new way to regain his self-respect and tranquility.

Selfless giving can be a source of comfort and reward.

chapters.title: Change doesn't ask if you're ready
chapters.text:
Scott aimed to transform his life by breaking free from his bad habits. He tried to quit drinking, smoking, gambling, and drugs. But all this was not enough; he needed a more drastic transformation. He prayed for God's guidance in finding a new path in life.

Don't be fooled by perfect success stories. In reality, they often result from a chain of errors and setbacks.

For Scott Harrison, the threat to his life was the point of no return. He wanted to become worthy and reasoned that he could achieve this by serving those in need. He had wasted ten years of his youth and promised himself that he would devote the next year to helping others. He sent out applications to participate in every humanitarian mission he could find. Naturally, charities didn't particularly need an irresponsible partygoer and addict. He received multiple rejections until Mercy Ships finally contacted him.

The organization sent volunteer doctors on hospital ships to countries with poor living standards to perform tumor removal surgeries for people who do not have access to medical care. The ship was sailing to West Africa at the time, and the company invited Scott as a correspondent and photographer.

For Scott Harrison, this was a test of strength. He had to live in poor conditions, without personal space, with strict discipline and restrictions. Moreover, he had to find the resolve to look at the face of poverty, misfortune, and suffering. He wasn't sure he was ready, but the opportunity to be a better person was long-awaited and desired. It was a true blessing, and Scott accepted it with gratitude.

Your feeling of being unprepared is exactly how God can use you best. ~ Scott Harrison

He embarked on a four-month journey to Benin, where the company's medical team planned to perform 1,500 surgeries. Upon reaching the shore, thousands of sick people, including children, were already waiting. They all had distorted body parts and deformed organs, and their skin displayed sores and swelling. Just two weeks prior, word had spread from settlement to settlement about the arrival of volunteer surgeons, prompting many desperate patients to walk for several days for assistance.

Did you know? In the early 2000s, there were only four doctors for every 100,000 people in Benin, resulting in an average life expectancy of less than 50 years. In contrast, the current life expectancy has risen to 62 years.

chapters.title: Hope is your best weapon in the face of pain
chapters.text:
As doctors examined the patients, Scott captured everything on camera. He made portraits of teenagers who could not swallow or speak because of the massive tumors in their mouths. Only a fraction of them made it onto the ship for surgery — they were the lucky ones with benign tumors. Others, however, were less fortunate. The most painful experience for every volunteer was refusing to help those diagnosed with cancer when their condition had progressed too far, and surgeons could not assist them. In other words, for doctors, this meant admitting their inability and informing patients of their imminent death.

Scott was looking for an answer from medical professionals about how they can endure such pain, come to terms with suffering, and where they find strength. And they gave him advice that guided him throughout his subsequent philanthropic work: to focus on hope.

Concentrate your thoughts and efforts on aiding those within your reach and let go of those you cannot help.

The local population suffered from diseases primarily due to malnutrition, dehydration, and consumption of bacteria-contaminated water. When Scott first watched African teenagers spend hours digging a hole to reach a well, he did not yet realize the importance of water to these people. Available sources of clean water can radically change an entire country's life and spur the next generations' development at a completely different pace. It would significantly reduce their health issues and free up time for education, work, and community development, instead of spending hours each day carrying water from a well to their homes.

Scott stayed with Mercy Ships for a year longer than planned and visited several African countries. Throughout his travels, he maintained a blog describing everything he saw and published photographs of patients before and after surgeries. Using his contact base from his clubbing days, Scott created a mailing list to draw the attention of wealthy, privileged individuals to underdeveloped countries' problems and inspire donors to donate. In New York City, he organized several major exhibitions and charity events that raised money for new medical missions to West Africa.

However, Scott felt he wasn't doing enough. Merely documenting other people's pain is insufficient when they require substantial assistance. Actions, not words, are an accurate measure of faith.

chapters.title: Fear shouldn't dictate your actions
chapters.text:
Dirty water has killed more people than all the wars in history. It destroys a living organism more devastatingly than all diseases combined. However, there is a cure. And, as a rule, it even exists in the poorest villages, hidden beneath the surface.

Scott Harrison found inspiration in giving people this antidote by building wells with automatic pumps. So, he launched his foundation, heading it with the symbolic words “charity: water.” Then the hard work began.

First of all, it was necessary to attract sponsors. For this, Scott had to make several trips to Uganda and Liberia to collect visual material illustrating the life of the local population. It was possible to convince the public of the need for donations only by showing them real people, their troubles, and their needs. The foundation had to win the trust of society to enlist their support.

Trust is a scarce resource. ~ Scott Harrison

Scott used every means possible to raise the necessary funds to construct wells. He organized charity events, parties, and exhibitions. His idea of selling bottles of water for twenty times their original cost and giving all the profits to the fund was wildly successful.

One well should serve a maximum of 250 people. Uganda has ministered to more than 30,000 people.

Scott felt overwhelmed when he considered the massive investments required to improve the drinking water situation in African countries. He was afraid that he wouldn't be able to handle it. But then he found comfort in the thought that Mercy Ships wouldn't have been able to save numerous lives if they had been intimidated by the workload at the start. He knew that God did not require him to complete all the tasks; he only needed to care for one problem at a time and rely on the universe's mercy. His labors and prayers were not in vain, and other enthusiasts soon shared his idea.

And now, 17 years later, Scott Harrison and his team have installed 120,000 drinking water sources in 29 countries. They aim to ensure that one hundred million people have access to clean water. It is a perfect example of faith put into action.

Did you know? The word charity comes from the Latin caritas that means to love, that is, to do good to others unselfishly.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
One of Scott Harrison's most valuable acquisitions on his charity trips was “African time.” It is the feeling of living in the present moment. For West Africans, there is no past — they cannot find it in photos and videos; it lives only in the memory of the oldest people in the settlement. There is also no future; no one knows what tomorrow will bring, so the locals do not make plans. They live only “now,” which allows them not to worry about the future and not to regret the past but to appreciate the joy available to them currently.

Scott Harrison's story illustrates that regardless of a person's past mistakes, they can transform their lives and find their purpose. Service and selfless help to those in need are blessings for both sides of these actions. No drug can provide the happiness and satisfaction that a person gets from realizing that they saved and improved the lives of others.

Scott taught society to perceive charity in a new way. He did not want to seek donations by drawing on feelings of guilt and pity. Instead, he gave people a sense of pride in their generosity and inspired them with the opportunity to change the world for the better. And, of course, as a former partygoer, he entertained them at charity parties, thereby creating a stable narrative in the minds of the masses that kindness equals the greatest joy.

Try this


• Watch the thought-provoking documentary “Above Water,” filmed by Aïssa Maïga (2021).
• Visit the site charitywater.org and donate what you can. Feel free to donate small amounts, as every little bit counts.
• Uncover the link between WASH (water, sanitation, and hygiene) and physical and sexual assaults targeting women. This issue includes instances of rape while collecting water from remote locations, domestic violence from disputes concerning the amount of water collected, and conflicts over the scarce water supply.

Read it till the end?
